input.headline: The Great Read Don’t Tread on Frank: How One Man’s Yard Became a Battlefield A dispute over HOA rules in a D.C. suburb escalated into a hyperlocal version of the nation’s partisan divide.

raw.headline: The Great Read Don’t Tread on Frank: How One Man’s Yard Became a Battlefield A dispute over HOA rules in a D.C. suburb escalated into a hyperlocal version of the nation’s partisan divide.

winner: The Great Read Don’t Tread on Frank was so intense, even the lawn gnomes took sides — one shouted “No yard art!” while the other yelled, “This is a *very* tiny statue of liberty.

candidates.text: Why did Frank finally give up on his yard war? He realized he couldn’t *read* between the lines anymore—everyone else was just *treading* on his lawn!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Smart wordplay on 'read' and 'tread,' ties directly to the headline, concise and surprising."}

candidates.text: The HOA called it "Don’t Tread on Frank." The homeowner just smiled and said, “Good, I’ve been meaning to ask—where *do* you want me to tread?
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'Don’t Tread on Me' with a twist of specificity and natural flow."}

candidates.text: The HOA's new rule is "No treading on Frank." Everyone just stands there awkwardly, one foot in the grass, one foot in the air, waiting for someone else to move first.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'Don’t Tread on Me' flag with 'No treading on Frank,' and the visual of awkward foot placement adds humor and specificity."}

candidates.text: The Great Read Don’t Tread on Frank was so intense, even the lawn gnomes took sides — one shouted “No yard art!” while the other yelled, “This is a *very* tiny statue of liberty.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising and specific twist with lawn gnomes taking sides, ties well to the headline and context."}

candidates.text: The Great Read Don’t Tread on Frank: How One Man’s Yard Became a Battlefield — it sounds like the start of a very long HOA meeting.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'The Great Read' and 'Don’t Tread on Me,' with a clever nod to HOA meetings and the headline’s tone."}

candidates.text: The HOA president tried to cite Frank for his "Don't Tread on Me" flag, but Frank just told him to take a hike because he was tired of people stepping on his lawn's liberties.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 7.5, "reason": "The joke is timely, specific, and ties the headline to a humorous twist on the situation. It uses wordplay ('liberties' vs. 'lawn') effectively, but could be more surprising or concise."}

candidates.text: Frank tried to plant a garden, but his HOA said no—so he grew a lawn full of Republicans and Democrats, just to show them who’s really in charge.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and ties into the headline's theme, but it's a bit generic and lacks specificity. The pun on 'grew a lawn full of Republicans and Democrats' is clever, but could be more tightly connected to the hyperlocal partisan divide mentioned in the headline."}

candidates.text: When the HOA demanded he remove the "Don't Tread on Me" snake from his lawn, Frank insisted it was a matter of property rights, but the neighbors were more concerned that the snake was technically a violation of the "no unauthorized reptiles" clause.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist with the 'no unauthorized reptiles' clause, specific to HOA rules, concise and funny."}

candidates.text: The HOA eventually sued Frank for his "Don’t Tread on Me" flag, but the court ruled that since the flag was technically a threat of violence, it was the only thing in the neighborhood allowed to be considered a public nuisance.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on legal interpretation, specific to HOA rules, concise and cleverly ties into the headline's theme."}
